era_context: The early reign of Tiberius (14–37 CE) in the Roman Empire, a period of consolidation and military campaigns. In China, Wang Mang's Xin dynasty was in its early years (9–23 CE), attempting administrative reforms. In India, the Satavahana Empire dominated the Deccan. In Mesoamerica, the Classic Maya civilization was developing, though writing and monumental architecture were still in early stages. This was a period of relative stability in the Mediterranean world following Augustus's reign.

events.title: Tiberius consolidates power in Rome
events.description: In the years following his accession in 14 CE, Tiberius was consolidating his authority over the Senate and the military. By 4 CE (counting backward from his accession), this would be during Augustus's final years, but the transition of power was already being prepared. Tiberius was serving as a trusted military commander and heir apparent. The period was marked by careful political maneuvering to ensure a smooth succession and to establish Tiberius's legitimacy as Augustus's chosen successor.

sources.name: Suetonius, Life of Augustus and Life of Tiberius

events.certainty_note: Augustus's reign dates (27 BCE – 14 CE) are well-established. The political dynamics of his final years are described in multiple sources, though all are written decades or centuries later. The specific events of 4 CE are not individually documented; this entry describes the broader context of the succession.

events.title: Continued Roman military operations in the Balkans and Danube frontier
events.description: Roman legions were engaged in ongoing military operations to secure the Danube frontier and suppress unrest in the Balkans. These campaigns were part of Augustus's broader strategy to establish stable frontiers and consolidate control over recently conquered territories. The Danube region was strategically important for controlling trade routes and preventing incursions from the north. Military operations in this region would continue throughout Tiberius's reign.
key_figures: Roman military commanders (names largely unrecorded for specific 4 CE operations)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Danube forts and camps

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Roman military activity on the Danube frontier during Augustus's reign is well-documented. Specific events of 4 CE are not individually attested, but the broader context of frontier consolidation is clear from archaeological evidence and later historical accounts.
cross_references: 6-001 (Pannonian and Dalmatian revolts, which may have roots in earlier unrest)

events.title: Wang Mang's administrative reforms in China
events.description: Wang Mang, who had usurped the throne in 9 CE, continued implementing administrative and economic reforms designed to restore what he believed were ancient Zhou practices. These reforms included land redistribution, price controls, and the establishment of state monopolies on salt and iron. However, these policies were generating significant resistance from landowners and merchants, and natural disasters (droughts and floods) were exacerbating social unrest. The combination of policy disruption and environmental stress was creating conditions for the peasant rebellions that would eventually topple his dynasty.

sources.name: Han Shu (History of the Han), compiled by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: Wang Mang's reign and general policies are well-documented in Chinese sources. The Han Shu was compiled within a century of events. However, the specific timeline of individual reforms and their effects in 4 CE is not precisely documented; this entry describes the broader context of his reign.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Wang Mang as a well-intentioned reformer has been significantly revised by modern scholarship, which emphasizes the destabilizing and economically damaging effects of his policies. The extent to which specific reforms can be dated to 4 CE versus other years in his reign is uncertain, as sources do not provide precise chronologies. The characterization of Tiberius's succession as smooth and uncontested has been questioned by some scholars, who point to tensions and uncertainties in the transition.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 4 CE is sparse and dominated by Roman and Chinese sources. Most accounts of this year are reconstructed from broader narratives of the periods in which they fall. Roman sources are biased toward military and political narratives of the elite. Chinese sources are often hostile to Wang Mang, portraying him as a usurper and his policies as failures. The absence of contemporary written records for most of the world means that this year is essentially invisible in the historical record outside the Mediterranean and China.

graph_edges.note: Tiberius's consolidation of power as heir apparent led to his accession in 14 CE
Wang Mang's reforms and the social unrest they generated contributed to the eventual collapse of his dynasty
Ongoing frontier tensions may have contributed to the Pannonian and Dalmatian revolts
